evidence: Ivor's framing: 'The value they get in the first six months will be like... yeah. The value they'll have in six years time if they keep up with it will be wild. Those people won't just be users, they'll be like fans and advocates.'
V expanded Ivor's framing into mental model: 'Career infrastructure, not career tool. Like a life operating system.'

V's framing: 'Academies have their local towns, regions, catchment areas locked the fuck down. And that is why you can reliably generate talent. If you aren't able to do that, you don't survive as an academy.'
Implication: 'The company that owns a university's career pipeline owns 10 years of talent flow from that institution.'

source_display: The Black Pill on Talent Visibility
evidence: V's framing: 'Companies will never invest in true AI for resume scanning. Because why would you process a resource where the ratio is like 99 crap for 1% signal. And even that could be totally fake now.'

source_display: The Black Pill on Talent Visibility
evidence: Dark implication: 'In a world of AI-generated everything, the only reliable signal is direct interaction over time.' Extends the resume-signal-decay thesis to full visibility collapse.
Ivor: 'let me see who else I know at Handshake' - actively searched LinkedIn during call; has existing annotation services relationship with them

source_display: Pursue Handshake Introduction via Ivor's Network
evidence: Ivor's new Head of Strategic Ops will reach out after joining (week after next)

source_display: The Quiet Part Problem as Market Opportunity
evidence: Employers have selection criteria they cannot legally state. The gap between stated job requirements and actual selection preferences is a structural feature of hiring markets, not a bug.

source_display: Information Efficiency Has Inverted Recruiter Value
evidence: Indian recruiter placing US engineers: 'I have nothing to give these people besides the resume. All of these people look the same.' Commoditization already happened; AI just made it visible.

source_display: Response Rate as the Only Metric That Matters
evidence: Logan's insight: Use Careerspan's high response rates as the proxy metric for convincing recruiters that 'people pay attention to our recommendations.'

source_display: Talent Scanning as Core Value Proposition
evidence: V: 'What we are is talent scanning, and that's literally the only thing that matters.' Reframes product priorities, marketing messaging, and technical roadmap.

source_display: Talent Scanning as Core Value Proposition
evidence: Distillation of enrichment layer thesis to single core: talent scanning is the only value prop that matters, all else ladders up to it.
Creates 'strong indication of them actually being interested' - validates response rate metrics and protects candidate agency

source_display: The Sora Video as Product Vision
evidence: Ilse's recollection of generated Sora video—auditorium full of candidates empties to three after clicking Careerspan button—articulates value prop: 'hiring is too loud, and we make it quiet.'
In talent marketplaces, having the name of a prestigious firm on your website may be worth more than their actual business contribution. The brand IS the product.
V's counter to 'you only have 500 users': 'Yes, but they're the right 500.' Inverts network effects playbook to exclusivity positioning.
Logan's biotech friend disrupted industry by going directly to morgues. Provocative question: Who is Careerspan's middleman that could be bypassed?
Pursue both brand-name partnerships (Superposition) for credibility AND hungry individual recruiters for execution. V owns Superposition, Logan owns individuals.
Prioritize NYC for high-signal user acquisition: Harvard Club, Cornell Club, AI Circle via Albert
Ilse pushed back: 'They won't bring users' and 'everything you just said is also true of any recruiter'
